topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my phone, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the quiet hum of my afternoon. Sarah was moving. Across the country. I let my thumb hover over the screen for a full minute, the inertia of my current state holding me firmly in place on the sofa. A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, a sound that seemed to carry the weight of all my current obligations. I didn't bother to open the message immediately. Instead, I just stared at the dust motes dancing in the sliver of sunlight cutting across the worn carpet. It felt like a monumental effort, this news, and frankly, my energy reserves were already depleted for anything requiring significant exertion. I finally tapped the notification, reading the details with a detached sort of resignation. The sheer scale of the undertaking just made me want to sink deeper into the cushions.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I mumbled, dragging my feet across the cool wooden floor of my apartment. The news about Alex relocating felt like a sudden, unwelcome weight settling squarely on my chest. I didn't even bother changing out of my oversized hoodie; the thought of putting on anything else felt like climbing a mountain. I slumped against the doorframe, letting my head drop back against the frame, feeling a deep, satisfying slump in my shoulders. Why engage? Why process this seismic shift in our friendship? It was just too much input for a brain that was currently operating at minimum capacity. I picked up a remote and started scrolling aimlessly through streaming services, the remote feeling heavy and cumbersome in my hand.

[story 3]

When I finally read the email confirming Sarah’s move, I just blinked. The information swam before my eyes, indistinct and distant. A profound sense of inertia washed over me, a desire to simply cease movement altogether. I didn't move from my chair, the springs groaning softly under my stillness. It was an enormous undertaking for her, I supposed, but watching her chase this new horizon felt exhausting just by observing it. I let my gaze drift to the window, where the city seemed to blur into an indistinct backdrop. The effort required to formulate a response, to even muster the energy for a proper reaction, seemed insurmountable at that moment. I decided, quite firmly, that nothing needed to be done right now.
